title: NOC 75100 – Longshore workers | GTR Immigration
description: NOC 75100 professionals are the indispensable manual force driving Canada’s global trade. As a TEER 3 occupation (formerly Skill Level C), Longshore workers transfer cargo between ships and shore-based transport, such as trucks and railcars. In 2026, as Canada expands its "Indo-Pacific Strategy" and upgrades its major ports in Vancouver, Prince Rupert, and Montreal, these workers are classified as Essential Supply Chain Talent. For immigration, this role is a key target for the New 2026 TR to PR Pathway and various Provincial Nominee Programs (PNPs).

classification.teer: Classification LevelDetailsBroad Occupation Category7 – Trades, transport and equipment operators and related occupationsTEER Level3Major Group75 – Helpers and labourers and other transport drivers, operators and labourersSub-Major Group751 – Longshore workers and material handlersMinor Group7510 – Longshore workers and material handlersNOC Code75100

duties: Cargo Handling: Operate power winches or drive drive-on/drive-off (Ro-Ro) vehicles to load and unload ship cargo such as automobiles, machinery, and livestock.
Loading & Unloading: Manually or with mechanical aids, load and unload grain, coal, or other bulk materials into or from ship holds.
Stowing: Secure and shore up cargo in ship holds using dunnage, lashings, and bracing to prevent shifting during transit.
Equipment Operation: Operate specialized terminal equipment like forklifts, front-end loaders, and small cranes to move cargo within the terminal area.
Signaling: Provide hand signals or radio instructions to crane operators to ensure safe and precise movement of containers and heavy lifts.
Maintenance: Assist with basic maintenance of terminal equipment and ensure the dock area is clear of hazards.
employment_requirements: Some secondary school education is typically required.
On-the-Job Training: Extensive on-the-job training is provided, often through union-led apprenticeship or "casual" worker programs.

Certifications:Most ports require a Marine Security Clearance (Transport Canada).Specialized equipment operators require internal port certifications for forklifts, cranes, or heavy machinery.First Aid and WHMIS (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System) are standard.
Most ports require a Marine Security Clearance (Transport Canada).
Specialized equipment operators require internal port certifications for forklifts, cranes, or heavy machinery.
First Aid and WHMIS (Workplace Hazardous Materials Information System) are standard.
Physical strength, the ability to work in all weather conditions, and a willingness to work on-call or in shifts are essential.
salary_prospects: Note: In 2026, experienced Longshore workers in major BC ports often earn annual incomes between $95,000 and $130,000 inclusive of overtime and shift premiums.
job_outlook: The outlook is Favourable. Projections indicate a steady increase in maritime trade as Canada diversifies its global partnerships. While port automation is increasing, the need for skilled workers to oversee, troubleshoot, and manage the "human-in-the-loop" aspects of container logistics ensures strong job security for the next decade.
labour_market_demand: The demand for Longshore workers in 2026 is High to Very High in Canada’s coastal and Great Lakes provinces. In British Columbia, the expansion of the Roberts Bank Terminal 2 project has created a massive surge in hiring. In Quebec and Nova Scotia, the modernization of container terminals has increased the need for workers capable of managing high-volume, automated loading systems. According to 2026 port authority data, the industry is currently experiencing a 15% labor shortage due to record-breaking cargo volumes.
The Gateway to the World: Work in world-class facilities like the Port of Vancouver, Canada's largest and most diversified port.
Exceptional Union Benefits: Most longshore workers are represented by strong unions (e.g., ILWU), ensuring some of the highest hourly wages for manual labor in North America.
High-Tech Logistics: Gain experience with semi-automated gantry cranes, reach stackers, and real-time digital terminal operating systems (TOS).
Direct PR Pathway: This TEER 3 role is a primary beneficiary of the New 2026 TR to PR Pathway, which targets essential supply chain workers already in Canada.
pathways: New 2026 TR to PR Pathway: A dedicated stream for 33,000 workers already in Canada, with a high quota specifically for supply chain and transport workers.
BC PNP Entry-Level and Semi-Skilled (ELSS): A direct route for longshore workers currently employed in British Columbia.
Nova Scotia Occupations in Demand: Frequently targets marine terminal workers to support the Port of Halifax.
Atlantic Immigration Program (AIP): A high-conversion route for those with a job offer from a designated employer in an Atlantic port.
